nativeRange.habitat: Slow-moving and standing water: medium to large rivers, flooded fields, rice paddies, and streams; often in areas with soft substrate and dense vegetation or leaf litter

decorPreferences: fine sand substrate (mandatory — coarse gravel injures scaleless skin)
driftwood and smooth rocks forming cave-like hiding spots
PVC pipes or hollow ornaments as refuge tubes
Indian almond leaf litter to tint water and reduce stress
dense planting (Java fern, Anubias, Vallisneria) for cover

diet.notes: The Half-Banded Spiny Eel is a specialized micro-carnivore that strongly prefers live or frozen invertebrates. Staple foods include bloodworms, blackworms, mosquito larvae, daphnia, brine shrimp, and mysis shrimp; larger adults will also accept finely chopped earthworm, krill, or prawn. The species feeds nocturnally, so food should be offered after lights go out. Dry or prepared foods are typically refused at first, though patient target-feeding with a pipette near the burrow entrance may eventually coax acceptance of high-quality sinking pellets. Feeder fish should be avoided, as they introduce significant disease risk.

goodWith: hatchetfish (surface swimmers that won’t compete for the bottom)
mid-water tetras of 4 cm or larger
angelfish and other peaceful cichlids too large to be eaten
avoidWith: fish smaller than ~4 cm (will be consumed at night)
neon tetras and similarly sized nano fish
aggressive or fin-nipping species (tiger barbs, serpae tetras)
other bottom-dwelling predators competing for the same territory
large cichlids that harass bottom fish
any copper-based medication in the tank

imageCandidates.notes: Photo by Zakqary Roy, 1080x810. Live specimen in a container showing the elongate scaleless eel body, tubular pointed snout and dorsal spine ridge with tan/brown coloration; correct ID. Only clearly-live CC-friendly image found; amber lighting and slight glass haze but in acceptable focus with whole visible animal framed.

summary: The Half-Banded Spiny Eel (Macrognathus circumcinctus) is one of the smaller spiny eels, reaching around 15–20 cm. Native to the Mekong and Chao Phraya basins of Southeast Asia, it is completely scaleless and highly sensitive to copper medications. Fine sand is essential, and its reputation as a determined escape artist means the tank must have a tightly sealed lid.
careNotes:
The Half-Banded Spiny Eel is one of the more manageable Mastacembelidae, reaching around 15–20 cm in the aquarium compared to the much larger Tire Track Eel (M. armatus, fw-fish-133). It is nonetheless an intermediate-level fish due to its specific substrate, diet, and security requirements.

Substrate is non-negotiable: fine sand of at least 5 cm depth must be provided. The species is completely scaleless, and even moderately coarse gravel will abrade its skin within days, leading to bacterial infections and death. Smooth river sand or pool-filter sand are ideal; sharp silica sand should be avoided. Decorations must be placed on the tank base before adding the sand so the eel’s burrowing cannot undermine them and cause crushing injuries.

Escape risk is extremely high. Any gap larger than the diameter of a pencil in the lid is sufficient for an escape — these eels have been documented squeezing through filter inlet openings and overflow boxes. All openings must be sealed with foam or mesh. A lid check should be part of routine maintenance.

Being nocturnal, the eel rarely emerges during the day; new keepers sometimes fear the fish is missing or dead when it is simply buried. Feed after lights-out, ideally with a dim moonlight or red-light to allow observation. A pipette or turkey baster can be used to deliver food near a known burrow entrance.

Water quality should be stable with soft, slightly acidic to neutral chemistry matching its native Mekong/Chao Phraya lowland rivers. Weekly water changes of 20–25% are recommended. As a scaleless species it is highly sensitive to copper-based treatments (e.g., Cupramine, CopperSafe) — alternative medications must be used for parasitic treatment. Praziquantel and fenbendazole are generally tolerated. Indian almond leaves and peat filtration to maintain a light tannin tint are beneficial.

The species can be kept singly or in small groups in suitably sized aquaria; conspecific aggression is low when sufficient hiding spots are provided. Distinguished from the Lesser Spiny Eel (M. siamensis, fw-fish-200) by its more regular, fully-oblique bar pattern, and from the Tire Track Eel (M. armatus, fw-fish-133) by its substantially smaller adult size and simpler half-bar markings rather than the complex reticulated track pattern.
breedingNotes: Breeding in the home aquarium is extremely rare and poorly documented. The species is believed to be an egg-scatterer, depositing adhesive eggs in fine-leaved plants or substrate. Simulated rainy-season conditions — a period of lower water level and temperature followed by a water change with slightly cooler, soft water — is thought to trigger spawning. Live food feeding should be maximised in the lead-up to attempted breeding. Fry, if obtained, are very small and require infusoria or similar micro-foods as first foods. Species-only breeding tank strongly recommended.

primary.name: FishBase — Macrognathus circumcinctus
primary.notes: Primary taxonomic and range source. Confirmed family Mastacembelidae, order Synbranchiformes; native range Mekong and Chao Phraya basins, southeastern Thailand, Malay Peninsula, and Sumatra, Indonesia; temperature 24–27°C; nocturnal invertebrate and small-fish predator; demersal bottom dweller. FishBase lists max size 28.6 cm TL — this likely represents a large outlier or includes closely related taxa; hobbyist sources consistently report 15–20 cm in the aquarium trade.

additional.name: Fishkeeper.co.uk — Half Banded Spiny Eel
additional.notes: Provided temperature 24–27°C, pH 6.0–7.0, hardness up to 15 dH, adult size to 20 cm, diet (frozen mosquito larvae, brine shrimp, daphnia, mysis), compatible tankmates (large rasboras, gouramis, peaceful loaches, Geophagus, L-number plecos), soft sand substrate requirement, no captive breeding records confirmed, native range Cambodia/Indonesia/Malaysia/Thailand.

additional.name: AquAnswers — Half-Banded Spiny Eel
additional.notes: Hobbyist care guide; confirmed scaleless copper sensitivity, high escape risk with tight-lid requirement, nocturnal/crepuscular feeding behaviour, 6–10 year lifespan, 30-gallon (120 L) minimum tank, bloodworms/brine shrimp/earthworm diet, peaceful temperament with larger tankmates.

additional.name: StudioScaped — Half-Banded Spiny Eel
additional.notes: Confirmed adult size 15–20 cm, temperature 23–28°C, 30-gallon (120 L) minimum, carnivore preferring frozen/live bloodworms, mysis shrimp, brine shrimp, chopped earthworm; peaceful temperament; fine sand substrate critical; high escape risk; copper sensitivity confirmed.

additional.name: AquaInfo — Macrognathus circumcinctus
additional.notes: Temperature 24–28°C, pH 7.0–7.5, adult size up to 20 cm (typically 12 cm in captivity); fine sandy bottom mandatory; diet Artemia and mosquito larvae; avoid fish under 3 cm; Mekong/Chao Phraya basin, southeastern Thailand, Sumatra.

fish.breedingNotes: Breeding in captivity is extremely rare, with no reliably documented home-aquarium spawnings on record. The species is believed to scatter adhesive eggs among fine-leaved plants or substrate. Simulated rainy-season conditioning — a water change with cooler, softer water following a period of lower water level — combined with heavy live-food feeding is thought to encourage spawning. Any fry obtained are very small and require infusoria or similar micro-foods as their first food. Breeding attempts are best made in a species-only tank.
